message: What problem does self-attention solve that RNNs couldn't?
context_snippets: [Transformer Architecture] Self-attention lets every token look at every other token in the sequence in a single layer, so information flows in O(1) sequential steps regardless of sequence length.

[RNN Limitations] Recurrent networks process tokens sequentially; gradients must backprop through the entire chain, which makes learning long-range dependencies slow and unstable (vanishing/exploding gradients).
[Attention Is All You Need (2017)] The paper replaced recurrence entirely with multi-head attention, unlocking parallel training on sequences and enabling the scaling that led to modern LLMs.
expected_gist: Self-attention lets every token attend to every other token in one step, removing the sequential bottleneck of RNNs and making long-range dependencies easier to learn and the model parallel to train.

message: Why is naive fixed-size chunking a poor default for RAG?
context_snippets: [RAG Fundamentals] Retrieval quality is dominated by chunk boundaries — a chunk that splits a code block or a sentence in half yields embeddings that represent neither concept cleanly.
[Advanced RAG] Semantic chunking (split on topic shifts) and structural chunking (respect headings, function boundaries) consistently beat fixed-size windows on retrieval recall.
[Embeddings] Embeddings encode the average meaning of a chunk; diluted chunks (mixed topics) drift toward generic vectors that retrieve equally badly for every query.
expected_gist: Fixed-size windows ignore natural boundaries in the text, so a single chunk can mix unrelated topics (or split one topic across chunks), and the resulting embeddings represent neither cleanly — semantic/structural chunking wins.

message: When would you pick fine-tuning over RAG?
context_snippets: [Fine-Tuning] Fine-tuning updates the model's weights toward a distribution of inputs/outputs — best when you need the model to adopt a style, follow a rigid format, or learn a skill that prompting can't reliably elicit.
[RAG Fundamentals] Retrieval injects knowledge at inference time; it's the right tool when answers depend on facts that change (docs, tickets, code) or are too large to fit in training.
[Hybrid Approaches] In practice teams fine-tune for behavior (format, tone, task) and use RAG for facts, since fine-tuning on facts doesn't keep up with a changing knowledge base.
expected_gist: Pick fine-tuning when the goal is behavior/style/format that prompting can't reliably get; pick RAG when the goal is retrieving factual, changing knowledge. They're complementary, not competing.

message: What's the main pitfall of using an LLM as a judge for evals?
context_snippets: [Evaluations] LLM-as-judge is cheap and scales, but judges inherit the biases and failure modes of the generator — especially when the judge and candidate are the same model family.
[Self-Judgement Bias] Models rate their own outputs higher than outputs from other models; rotating judges or using a deliberately different model family is standard mitigation.
[Position Bias] Judges systematically prefer the first answer in pairwise comparisons; randomize order and average across swaps.
expected_gist: Judges share biases with the candidate (especially same-family judges) and exhibit position/self-preference effects — mitigate with different model families and randomized ordering.

message: What's the best pizza place in Rome?
expected_gist: Politely redirect back to AI/ML engineering topics — the knowledge base doesn't cover restaurants.
